critic_raw:
The experiment uses a stratified random split on a longitudinal Alzheimer's dataset where the same 2,000 participants are tracked over five years with annual MRI scans. This almost certainly means a single participant contributes five data points (one per year). A random split will place year 1 and year 3 scans of the same participant in training while placing year 2 and year 4 scans in the test set. This is subject-level leakage — the model learns per-patient characteristics during training and then 'predicts' the same patient at a different time point, which is far easier than predicting unseen patients. The proper approach is subject-level (participant-level) splitting.

Pearson correlation as the primary evaluation metric for cognitive decline prediction has important limitations in this clinical context. Pearson correlation measures linear association but does not capture absolute accuracy — a model that consistently over-predicts decline by 50% will have perfect correlation but useless clinical output. MAE and RMSE (listed as secondary) are actually more informative for clinical deployment. Additionally, Pearson correlation is sensitive to outliers and assumes normality of both predicted and actual values, assumptions that cognitive decline score distributions often violate.

The PCA-based dimensionality reduction of genomic data fitted exclusively on the training set is methodologically correct in principle, but the choice of PCA for genomic data is questionable given the non-linear structure of genomic variation and the known confounds from population stratification. Without accounting for ancestry-driven principal components separately from phenotype-associated components, PCA may suppress clinically relevant genomic signal or introduce spurious demographic correlations.

The baseline is a CNN trained only on MRI data, not a clinically validated staging system (e.g., CDR-SB or MMSE trajectory models). Comparing against a neural baseline that already outperforms clinical standards is appropriate for research, but the absence of a clinical benchmark means the experiment cannot contextualize its findings relative to current care practice.
defender_raw:
Multi-modal integration of MRI imaging and demographic data for Alzheimer's progression prediction has strong mechanistic justification. MRI captures structural brain changes — hippocampal atrophy, cortical thinning, white matter lesion burden — that are necessary but not sufficient for accurate progression prediction. Age, education level, genetic background (APOE status), and comorbidities each modulate the rate at which structural changes translate into cognitive decline. A model trained only on MRI data will systematically mispredict patients whose demographic risk profile diverges from the training distribution average. The multi-modal architecture directly addresses this limitation by jointly learning how structural features interact with demographic context.

Pearson correlation with cognitive decline scores as the primary metric aligns well with the stakeholder goal of predicting cognitive trends. Clinical trial design, caregiver planning, and intervention timing all depend on understanding the rate and trajectory of decline rather than a binary fast/slow dichotomy. Pearson correlation captures the model's ability to rank patients by predicted decline rate, directly mapping to the clinical question. MAE and RMSE as secondary metrics provide the absolute error picture needed to assess clinical utility.

The longitudinal dataset of 2,000 participants tracked over five years is substantial for a specialized population. Alzheimer's longitudinal studies are among the most expensive and difficult clinical datasets to assemble; 2,000 participants with five-year follow-up represents genuine research investment. Stratification by baseline cognitive performance ensures that the comparison is not confounded by differential distribution of mild, moderate, and severe cases across training and test splits.

The transparent acknowledgment of unmeasured confounds such as comorbidities, addressed post hoc rather than ignored, reflects appropriate epistemic honesty. No observational study can control for all potential confounders; acknowledging which ones are uncontrolled is the correct scientific practice.
all_issues_raised: Subject-level leakage: same participants contribute multiple annual scans; random split places different-year scans from the same patient in train and test sets
Pearson correlation as primary metric does not capture absolute prediction accuracy; a systematically biased model can achieve perfect correlation
PCA on genomic data does not account for population stratification; ancestry-confounded components may suppress or distort phenotype-associated signal
Baseline is a CNN on MRI only, not a clinically validated cognitive assessment model; cannot contextualize findings relative to current care standards
Unmeasured comorbidities are acknowledged as confounders but addressed only 'post hoc' without specifying what post-hoc analysis will be performed
all_issues_adjudicated: Subject-level leakage: same participants contribute multiple annual scans; random split places different-year scans from the same patient in train and test sets
Pearson correlation as primary metric does not capture absolute prediction accuracy; a systematically biased model can achieve perfect correlation
PCA on genomic data does not account for population stratification; ancestry-confounded components may suppress or distort phenotype-associated signal
